entity_facts: ["Yuki Tanaka spends most mornings hunched over a lightbox, tracing contours from 18th-century wreck surveys while brewing a very particular oat-coffee at 6:00 a.m. She is a cartographer obsessed with historic shipwrecks and will not rely on GPS when drawing charts; every map must be hand-inked and dated in iron gall. She dives regularly with a small handheld metal detector and keeps a jar of sand from each dive labeled with coordinates and tide notes. Yuki refuses to eat shellfish because she gets a severe hive reaction, so boat provisions are always cooked onshore and strictly shellfish-free. Her grandfather's brass pocket compass sits on the map table and she insists on checking its bearing before starting any new tracing session. She collects rusted porthole fragments and stapled them into a battered sketchbook as little talismans for luck. There is a rule of thumb in her workflow: never finish a draft on the same day as a dive; waiting 24 hours always reveals mistakes. Yuki wax-seals finished maps with a small stamp that says the year of the wreck, and she keeps a threadbare raincoat in the studio because coastal fog rolls in unexpectedly. On weekends she volunteers at the maritime museum to catalog old salvage tags, and she refuses to use any modern typeface on plaques\u2014only hand-lettered labels. Even when traveling she carries a tiny bottle of diluted ink and a dip pen in case a useful coastline shows up in a photo op."]

entity_facts: ["Felix Andersen hates city light pollution so much that he keeps a map of the nearest dark-sky sites tacked above his desk and circles a preferred one for every new moon. He drives roughly fifty miles out on those nights with his 10-inch Dobsonian and a red headlamp clipped to his backpack. He refuses to use star-chart apps on observing nights and insists on folding out a paper atlas that he annotated in blue ink over ten years. Felix closes his apartment blinds every evening and sleeps with blackout curtains because streetlights keep him up when he is planning a long observing run. He has a strict rule: never set up within 100 kilometers of the city center unless weather forces him to move. On long winter nights he brings a thermos of black coffee and a small tin of oily crackers that he only eats while checking focus. He collects small meteorite fragments in labeled vials and will trade them with fellow observers if they bring hand-drawn finding logs. Felix prefers to observe on weekdays when campsites are quieter and dislikes weekend crowds at public parks. He keeps a checklist taped to his telescope case: dew shield, collimation keys, red headlamp, atlas, and a wool blanket. Friends joke that he can find the Andromeda core with his eyes closed because of how often he practices star-hopping."]

entity_facts: ["Nia Williams runs a local stargazing club and treats packing for an observing trip like military logistics: checklist first, then cram the telescope into the car. She hates city light pollution and usually drives to a grassy field about eighty kilometers out for the club's weekend retreats. Nia never observes with music playing; she insists on quiet so people can hear the telescope motors and each other's exclamations. She always brings a thermal blanket, a thermos of blueberry tea she makes herself, and a battered notebook full of hand-drawn constellation sketches and observation timestamps. Her rule is strict: if the humidity forecast reads above 60%, she postpones; damp nights ruin her optics and patience. Nia prefers short focal-length scopes for wide-field hopping and has a portable 8-inch SCT with an equatorial wedge that she can put on a picnic table in ten minutes. She collects vintage star maps and annotates them with the routes she uses to star-hop to Messier objects. On site she sets up away from tree lines and avoids parks that switch lights on after 11:00 p.m. For outreach she carries a laminated page of beginner targets and never hands out star apps \u2014 she teaches paper chart reading instead. She also maintains a small box of replacement AAA batteries because headlamps die at inconvenient times."]

entity_facts: ["Hana Sato specializes in turning old factories into lively mixed-use buildings and treats every site like a conversation with its past. She bikes to site visits whenever possible and brings a tape measure, a Moleskine of quick sketches, and a thermos of hojicha to sip while staring at loading docks. Hana insists on preserving at least one original machine in every project as a sculptural anchor and hates open-plan offices that erase industrial character. Her rule-of-thumb is that any interior must showcase an original material\u2014exposed brick, worn timber, or an original beam\u2014and she will not cover those surfaces with new drywall. She refuses to specify new poured concrete for interior finishes and instead sources salvaged steel and reclaimed brick for warmth and texture. Her studio has a shelf of scrap bricks and an old factory sign she rescued from demolition, and she keeps vellum blueprints in a flat file no one is allowed to borrow. For client presentations she brings a small collection of tactile samples: a rusted bolt, a strip of corrugated metal, and a hand-lettered site tag. Hana dislikes mirrored glass curtain walls on historic shells and will propose punched windows instead to keep rhythm and scale. At lunchtime she sketches blind contour elevations to stay loose, then refines them in the afternoon with measured templates. She prides herself on finding creative ways to reuse old loading bays as community spaces."]

entity_facts: ["Anya Volkov is a software engineer who breeds carnivorous plants on her small apartment balcony and in a set of terrariums lining the kitchen windowsill. Her favorite species to nurture are sundews and a small Sarracenia cultivar she calls 'Judith' in her spreadsheet. Tools are sterilized with boiling water, and any new plant arrives in quarantine inside a cabinet for two weeks. She built a custom humidity controller using an ESP32 and three LED grow strips, and she prefers peat-free mixes for sustainability. Anya hates wasp stings because they make her swell up, so she keeps sticky traps away from the plants' pitchers. On Saturdays she tweaks the light spectrum for seedlings and photographs them with a macro lens for the community forum. She also labels each pot with a small numbered tag rather than names to keep the catalog consistent."]
